text: The surgeon mobilizes the entire frenoesophageal membrane, followed by the mobilization of the mediastinum and distal esophagus up to eight centimeters.

The surgeon identifies the diverticulum adherent to the pleura on the right side. A barium swallow schematic in the left upper screen shows the dissection orientation. The surgeon performs a circumferential dissection of the diverticulum, starting from the distal aspect and moving anteriorly toward the pericardium. The pleura is gently peeled away from the diverticulum, and posterior dissection is also performed.

The surgeon uses endoscopy to visualize the diverticulum and its relation to surrounding structures. Blunt dissection techniques are employed to carefully separate and remove adhesions between the diverticulum and adjacent tissues.
The robotic-assisted camera provides stable visualization as the surgeon performs mediastinal dissection, focusing on removing adhesions from the proximal aspect of the diverticulum.
The surgeon completes the separation of the diverticulum from surrounding structures, reaching its superior extent. The diverticulum is then delivered out of the mediastinum.

After adhesiolysis, the surgeon identifies the neck of the diverticulum, which spans from five to nine centimeters above the gastroesophageal junction. The diverticulectomy is performed using multiple endo-GI blue loads, with an endoscope serving as a bougie. The stapled area is then imbricated using vicryl sutures.

The surgeon performs a myotomy on the anterolateral wall, with the diverticulum located in the anteromedial position. The myotomy extends two centimeters onto the stomach and five centimeters above the gastroesophageal junction. The crura is approximated using interrupted silk sutures, followed by an anterior door fundoplication.
Intraoperative endoscopy reveals a well-opened gastroesophageal junction with a visible fundoplication valve. The endoscopic view confirms the absence of mucostomy.
